criteria.name: Multiple Definition Consistency
criteria.1: Numerator and denominator go to different claimholders (e.g., EV / Net Income), or multiples defined inconsistently across firms (mixing trailing and forward PE)
criteria.3: Numerator and denominator match claimholders, time period stated, but dilution treatment or accounting consistency not verified across all peers
criteria.5: All multiples precisely defined with matching claimholders, uniform time period (TTM or forward), consistent dilution treatment (diluted shares), and accounting adjustments documented for cross-firm comparability

criteria.name: Comparable Universe Quality
criteria.1: Peers selected only by industry classification with no regard for growth, risk, or profitability differences. Fewer than 5 peers or obviously mismatched firms included
criteria.3: Peers share the same industry and approximate size. Some screening for growth or profitability. 8-15 firms with brief rationale for inclusion
criteria.5: Peers screened on industry, size, growth rate, risk profile, business model, and margin structure. 15+ firms for regression. Exclusions documented with rationale. Selection criteria checklist completed

criteria.name: Distribution Analysis
criteria.1: Only the mean or a single peer is used as the comparison point. No sense of how the target fits within the distribution
criteria.3: Mean and median reported. Target's position noted (above or below median). Some mention of range or spread
criteria.5: Full distribution statistics (mean, median, quartiles, standard deviation, range, count). Skewness noted and median used as primary central tendency for skewed distributions. Target's percentile rank identified. Outliers flagged and treated appropriately

criteria.name: Fundamental Driver Linkage
criteria.1: Multiple compared to peers with no reference to what drives it. No mention of growth, risk, or profitability as explanatory variables
criteria.3: Primary fundamental driver identified (e.g., growth for PE, ROE for PBV). Some discussion of whether target's premium or discount is justified by fundamentals
criteria.5: Fundamental driver equation stated for each multiple. Target's position on each driver (growth, risk, profitability) compared to peers. Clear assessment of whether the multiple premium or discount is consistent with fundamentals. Every multiple explicitly connected to its DCF derivation

criteria.name: Simple Comparison Rigor
criteria.1: Target multiple compared to peer average with no adjustment for differences. Conclusion is simply 'above average = expensive'
criteria.3: Target compared to peer median with qualitative acknowledgment of growth or risk differences. Some adjustment attempted
criteria.5: Target compared to peer median with explicit qualitative adjustments for each fundamental driver (growth, risk, profitability). Adjusted fair multiple computed. Direction and approximate magnitude of each adjustment justified

criteria.name: Regression Quality
criteria.1: No regression attempted, or regression run with fewer than 10 observations, or regression variables inconsistent with fundamental driver theory
criteria.3: Regression run with 15+ observations and correct driver variables. R-squared and coefficients reported. Coefficient signs checked against economic intuition
criteria.5: Regression well-specified with appropriate driver variables, 15+ observations, R-squared and adjusted R-squared reported, coefficient signs match theory, t-statistics checked for significance, outlier sensitivity tested, prediction interval acknowledged. Limitations (multicollinearity, nonlinearity, sample size) discussed

criteria.name: Implied Value Calculation
criteria.1: No implied value calculated from the multiple analysis. Analysis stops at 'the multiple is high or low' without converting to a price
criteria.3: Predicted multiple converted to implied price for at least one multiple. Current price compared. Over/undervaluation percentage computed
criteria.5: Implied price computed from multiple methods (simple comparison and regression) and multiple multiples (PE, EV/EBITDA). For firm multiples, equity bridge correctly applied (subtract debt, add cash). Results summarized in a multi-multiple table with consistency check

criteria.name: Intrinsic vs. Relative Reconciliation
criteria.1: Relative valuation presented as the final word on value with no mention of its limitations or relationship to intrinsic valuation
criteria.3: Caveat noted that relative valuation is relative to peers, not absolute. Some mention that DCF would complement the analysis
criteria.5: Clear distinction between relative pricing (cheap vs. expensive relative to peers) and absolute valuation (above or below intrinsic value). Explicit caveat that sector-level mispricing is not captured. If DCF estimate is available, relative and intrinsic results compared and divergences explained

key_requirements: PE defined consistently (trailing or forward, diluted shares) across all peers
Peers screened for positive earnings; firms with negative EPS excluded
Fundamental driver equation stated: PE = Payout x (1+g) / (ke - g)
Regression includes expected growth and beta (or cost of equity) as independent variables
Cyclical earnings addressed: use normalized PE for cyclical industries
common_pitfalls: Mixing trailing and forward PE across firms
Using mean PE in a right-skewed distribution instead of median
Ignoring that high PE may reflect high growth, not overvaluation
Including firms with negative earnings (negative PE is meaningless)

key_requirements: Enterprise value correctly computed (Market Cap + Debt - Cash) for all firms
EBITDA consistently calculated (before or after lease adjustments applied uniformly)
Fundamental drivers identified: tax rate, depreciation/EBITDA, reinvestment, WACC, growth
Capex differences between firms acknowledged (EBITDA masks capex variation)
Regression includes growth, tax rate, and reinvestment rate as variables
common_pitfalls: Using book value of debt instead of market value in EV calculation
Ignoring that two firms with same EBITDA but different capex should trade at different multiples
Not adjusting for operating lease capitalization consistently
Comparing EV/EBITDA across industries with very different capital intensity

key_requirements: PBV defined using most recent book value per share
ROE is the primary regression variable (PBV = (ROE - g) / (ke - g))
Financial services firms valued using PBV, not EV/EBITDA
Book value accounting differences addressed (GAAP vs IFRS, intangible capitalization)
Firms with negative book value excluded
common_pitfalls: Using PBV without controlling for ROE differences
Applying PBV to firms where book value has little economic meaning (light-asset tech companies)
Ignoring that buybacks can shrink book value and inflate PBV without changing firm value
Comparing PBV across different accounting regimes without adjustment

key_requirements: Operating margin is the primary regression variable (margin is the dominant driver of EV/Sales)
Revenue defined consistently (net revenue, same fiscal period)
Used appropriately for negative-earnings firms where PE and EV/EBITDA are unavailable
Acknowledgment that low EV/Sales may reflect low margins, not undervaluation
Expected revenue growth included as secondary regression variable
common_pitfalls: Concluding a firm is 'cheap' on EV/Sales without checking its margins
Using EV/Sales for profitable firms where PE or EV/EBITDA would be more informative
Not controlling for margin differences across peers
Ignoring that EV/Sales is most useful for unprofitable or early-stage companies

Simple Peer Comparison.description: Qualitative comparison of target's multiple to peer median, with directional adjustments for fundamental differences
key_requirements: Peer median (not mean) used as the benchmark
Fundamental drivers compared: growth, risk, profitability
Qualitative adjustments stated with directional reasoning
Implied value calculated from adjusted multiple
Simple Peer Comparison.when_to_use: Small peer set (5-15 firms), quick analysis, or as sanity check alongside regression

Sector Regression.description: OLS regression of multiple against fundamental drivers across sector, producing a predicted multiple for the target
key_requirements: Sample of 15+ firms with consistent data
Driver variables match fundamental theory for the multiple
R-squared, coefficient signs, and t-statistics reported and interpreted
Predicted multiple computed and compared to actual
Limitations acknowledged (sample size, multicollinearity, nonlinearity)
Sector Regression.when_to_use: Sufficient peer set size (15+), quantitative rigor desired, or when qualitative comparison is insufficient to resolve whether premium/discount is justified

common_failure_modes.symptom: Enterprise value divided by net income, or price divided by EBITDA
common_failure_modes.detection: Check whether numerator and denominator go to the same claimholders (equity or firm)
common_failure_modes.fix: Equity multiples: price or market cap in numerator, equity metric in denominator. Firm multiples: EV in numerator, firm metric in denominator

common_failure_modes.failure: Mean used instead of median for skewed distributions
common_failure_modes.symptom: PE sector 'average' of 35x used as benchmark when median is 22x and a few outliers at 80x+ pull the mean up
common_failure_modes.detection: Compare mean to median. If they differ by more than 15-20%, the distribution is skewed
common_failure_modes.fix: Use median as the primary central tendency for PE and PBV distributions. Report both mean and median but base comparisons on the median

common_failure_modes.symptom: Conclusion that a firm is 'overvalued' at PE 30x vs. peer median 20x, without noting the firm's growth rate is double the peer median
common_failure_modes.detection: Ask whether the comparison controls for growth, risk, and profitability differences
common_failure_modes.fix: Write the fundamental driver equation. Compare the target's position on each driver to the peer median. Assess whether the multiple premium or discount is consistent with fundamentals

common_failure_modes.symptom: Regression with 8 firms, R-squared of 0.95 (overfitting), and coefficients that are not statistically significant
common_failure_modes.detection: Check sample size (N < 15) and degrees of freedom (N minus number of predictors < 10)
common_failure_modes.fix: Use simple peer comparison when sample size is below 15. If regression is desired, reduce the number of independent variables to preserve degrees of freedom

common_failure_modes.symptom: Concluding the stock is 'undervalued' based solely on being below the sector median multiple, without acknowledging the sector itself may be overpriced
common_failure_modes.detection: Check whether the conclusion distinguishes between 'cheap relative to peers' and 'below intrinsic value'
common_failure_modes.fix: State the conclusion as relative: 'The stock appears cheap relative to sector peers after controlling for growth and risk.' Add caveat about sector-level pricing and recommend cross-checking with intrinsic (DCF) valuation

common_failure_modes.failure: Missing equity bridge for firm multiples
common_failure_modes.symptom: EV/EBITDA regression produces predicted EV, but no conversion to implied equity value per share (debt not subtracted, cash not added)
common_failure_modes.detection: Check whether the final output is an implied share price or just an implied enterprise value
common_failure_modes.fix: For firm multiples: Implied Equity Value = Implied EV - Debt + Cash. Then divide by diluted shares to get implied price per share

common_failure_modes.symptom: Some peers use trailing PE, others forward PE. Some include lease-adjusted EBITDA, others do not
common_failure_modes.detection: Check the definition column or footnotes in the peer comparison table
common_failure_modes.fix: Standardize: choose one definition (e.g., trailing 12-month diluted PE) and apply it uniformly to all firms. Document adjustments made for consistency

common_failure_modes.symptom: Firms with negative earnings included in PE analysis, producing negative PE ratios that distort the distribution
common_failure_modes.detection: Check for negative values in the denominator column of the peer table
common_failure_modes.fix: Exclude firms with negative denominators from the multiple analysis. Document the number excluded and consider whether the high exclusion rate suggests a different multiple should be used
